passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview

passages.text:
the domestic labour

that purchased

the silence

that wrote every poem

that you've ever readAbby Paige, Selected Hoems

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Box: Learning objectives
passages.heading: Learning objectives
passages.text:
- Explain the many-to-many relationship between grammatical categories and syntactic functions

- Distinguish between lexical categories (nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc.) and phrasal categories (NPs, VPs, AdjPs, etc.)

- Identify the main syntactic functions: head, subject, modifier, determiner, complement, and object

- Apply diagnostic tests to distinguish adjectives from other lexical categories

- Recognize and analyze adjective phrases in various syntactic contexts

- Differentiate between pronouns, determinatives, and adjectives based on their grammatical properties

- Understand how definiteness works in English noun phrases

- Use category and function terminology accurately in grammatical analysis

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Introduction
passages.heading: Introduction
passages.text: With some understanding of the concepts of standards, Standard English(es), grammaticality, and normativity under our belts, I turn back to the ideas we started to develop in Chapter. For the reasons laid out in Chapter 2, as well as for practical reasons, the focus will be on Standard English in Chapter 3 and most of the book. Here, I'll explain how categories and syntactic functions interact (Section ) in Standard English. I'll then provide explanations of the following categories: adjectives and adjective phrases in Section, pronouns in Section, and determinatives and determinative phrases in Section.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview
passages.heading: Categories and functions: an overview
passages.text:
I'm in my front garden in late May. The garden is lush, and the peonies are particularly notable as they begin to set their buds. Soon, they'll bloom into large flowers. But a closer examination of their stems reveals that they aren't very thick or strong, despite being fairly long. In fact, these peonies grow to about three feet in height. Given the size of the blooms, which can rival that of a newborn's head, their slender stems often struggle to support the weight, and the flowers droop.

Stems play a crucial role in plants. They provide structural rigidity, ensuring the plant remains upright. In the case of these peonies, the stems might not be up to the task. But in the same garden, there's a rosebush. Its stems are sturdy and can easily support the weight of its flowers. While roses and peonies are different types (or categories) of plants, their stems serve a similar function: transporting nutrients and providing support.

But stems aren't the only plant parts with important roles. Consider the roots. While they anchor the plant into the ground, offering some structural support, their primary function is nutrient absorption. So, while roots prioritize nutrient absorption and offer some support, stems focus more on support while also aiding in nutrient transport.

In my garden, there's also a challenge with weeds, especially on the pathways. To combat this, I have spread mulch. Interestingly, it is made of wood, which was a tree's stem (or trunk and branches). In its original role, the wood provided structural support to the tree and aided in nutrient transport. Now, repurposed as mulch, its function has shifted to weed suppression and moisture retention in the garden. Different organisms might see other uses for this wood: insects might view it as a food source, while a salamander might see it as a potential home.

The garden serves as a microcosm, illustrating the multifunctionality of natural elements. We categorize plant parts into stems, roots, flowers, and leaves, and each of these categories can serve multiple functions, from structural support to nutrient transport. Similarly, in language, words belong to lexical categories: nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc. These categories serve various linguistic functions, from modification to heading a phrase or acting as a subject or determiner.

The key takeaway is the flexibility and multifunctionality inherent in both nature and language. There isn't always a one-to-one relationship between categories and functions: a single function can be realized by multiple categories, just as a single category can fulfill various roles.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Box: Check your understanding
passages.heading: Check your understanding
passages.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

- In the garden metaphor, what do stems represent in language?

- Can you think of a word that serves different functions in different sentences?

- Why might the same function be performed by different categories?

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Many-to-many relationships
passages.heading: Many-to-many relationships
passages.text:
There's no one-to-one relationship between categories and functions, and the belief that there is is akin to the [sec:fallacy-of-monosemy]fallacy of monosemy. If there were, then we would have a much easier time defining the categories. For instance, if nouns and only nouns functioned as subjects, then we could simply define a noun as any word that functions as a subject, and we wouldn't have to bother with the stuff about coming after the and a, adjective phrases as modifiers, plurals, and the rest of it (Section ). But that's not how language works.

This echoes the point that was being made in the soup sketch: no single criterion will define a category. And yet, assuming one-to-one relationships is more or less what traditional English school grammars have always tried to do. They say that an adjective is any word that modifies a noun. Here's an example from the 11th edition of the very popular The big blue book of grammar and punctuation (Straus et al., 2014).

A noun is a word or set of words for a person, place, thing, or idea (p. 1).

An adjective is a word or set of words that modifies (i.e., describes) a noun or pronoun. Adjectives may come before the word they modify (p. 15).

Examples:

That is a cute puppy.

She likes a high school senior.

Of course, this can't be right. A high school is a place (or a thing?), which should make high school a noun under the definition above, but in the example sentence, it's supposed to be an adjective. In other cases, such as the Trudeau government, Trudeau is a person but the word is, in the phrasing of The blue book of grammar and punctuation, modifying the noun government. So is Trudeau a noun or an adjective? Or is it in some kind of quantum superposition of being both at the same time? In fact, both high school and Trudeau are just nouns, full stop (check the properties in Section yourself if you doubt this).

This illustrates how we can better understand language by recognizing that the relationships between categories (like noun and adjective) and functions (like subject or modifier) are many-to-many. Traditional grammars often try to resolve this complexity with explanations like "it's a noun functioning as an adjective," but that's like saying that the woody stem of a rhododendron is "functioning as a rose stem," or that poppies' long, slender stems "function as peony stems." These stems all function as (better or worse) supports, but the only stem that "functions as a rose stem" is a rose stem. Claiming that a noun "functions as an adjective" is a category mistake: adjective is not a function.

So to be perfectly clear, almost all categories perform multiple functions, and almost all functions can be performed by more than one category. Noun phrases, being no exception, have multiple functions, and not every subject is a noun phrase. In fact, it's quite common for nouns to function as modifiers in NPs as Trudeau does in the Trudeau government.

Make a list of other noun–noun combinations (e.g., soccer ball, faculty office), where the first is a modifier and the second is the head, and then check the following examples.

- health care, world war, law enforcement, climate change, air force, family member, video clip, credit card, breast cancer, interest rate, justice department, tea party, blood pressure, Saturday night, security council, heart attack, task force, press conference, stock market, school district, death penalty

- cell phone, phone call, call centre, centre stage, stage manager, manager position, position paper, paper towel, towel rack

- community college, college student, student loan, loan guarantee, guarantee program, program director, director level, level change

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories
passages.heading: Categories
passages.text:
We have two fundamental types of categories: lexical categories and phrasal categories. These categories can often be identified out of context. For instance, if you see the word happy, you can categorize it as an adjective. You can look it up in a dictionary, and it will say "adjective". There is no need for any more context. In some cases, the category will be ambiguous, as we saw with verbs/nouns like run, jump, and walk in Section. It's not the case that each of these words belongs to two categories; the verb walk and the noun walk are different words, belonging to different lexical categories. It's simply the case that we may not have enough information to say which of the two words we're looking at in a particular case. It is clear, however, that walk is not an adjective, adverb, preposition, or anything other than a noun or a verb.

Functions, on the other hand, are relational. We can't ever look up a word in a dictionary and find out whether it's a modifier, a subject, a head, or a dependent. These functions can only be determined in a context by seeing how the phrases relate to those around them. A dictionary can tell you what a word is, but not what it is up to.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Box: Quick self-check
passages.heading: Quick self-check
passages.text:
Categories vs. functions: Can you give an example of:

- One word that belongs to the same category but serves different functions?

- Two different categories that can serve the same function?

(If you're stuck, think about the word run or how both nouns and pronouns can be subjects.)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Lexical categories
passages.heading: Lexical categories
passages.text: Lexical categories contain individual words. Usually, in English, these are set off by spaces in writing, although there are some words that contain an internal space (e.g., each other). So far, we have considered nouns and verbs. The other lexical categories we will look at in this book are adjectives, pronouns (a kind of noun), determinatives, prepositions, adverbs, coordinators, and subordinators. The only lexical category we won't eventually look at is interjections. (If you'd like to learn about them, I suggest starting with the Wikipedia entry for English interjections, for which, as of this writing, I am the primary author: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_interjections.)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Phrasal categories
passages.heading: Phrasal categories
passages.text:
Most of the lexical categories mentioned so far have a related phrasal category, as shown in Figure. For nouns, there are noun phrases (or NPs); for verbs there are VPs; for adjectives, AdjPs, etc. There's one additional phrasal category, clause, which is headed by a VP as we saw in Section.

Figure: Some lexical and phrasal categories

As we saw in chapter 1, the everyday meaning of phrase happens to be a group of words that have a particular meaning when used together, but for our purposes, all we need is a head word. Often, this comes with dependents, but one-word phrases are also possible. This is another example of the difference between the everyday meaning and the technical definition. So, here, go to school is a VP, but so is go in please, go. We can have a long NP such as all the very interesting people who I met yesterday, but we can also have single-word NPs such as they or water.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions
passages.heading: Syntactic functions
passages.text:
Syntactic functions (or just functions) are broadly split into Head and Dependent. There is only one kind of head, but we'll divide dependents into Subject, Modifier, Determiner, and Complement. We'll also include a special kind of complement called an Object. There are some other functions that I'll introduce later, but we'll stick with these for now.

Once again, the following explanations may include terms that you're not yet familiar with. As I've said, because this is a system, this is difficult to avoid, but the terms will be explained soon, at which point, you may wish to review these definitions.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Head
passages.heading: Head
passages.text:
The head is the function of the most important element in a phrase. The phrase is almost always named after the lexical category of its head (e.g., an AdjP has an adjective as its head), and every phrase has a head. Heads are often the only element in a phrase. For instance, in rain fell, the subject NP contains just the head noun rain and the head VP contains just the head verb fell.

I will often shorten Head to H.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > What counts as a dependent
passages.heading: What counts as a dependent
passages.text:
A dependent is any element in the structure of a phrase or clause other than the head: the best kind (NP headed by kind with two dependents); won an award (VP headed by won with one dependent); more important (AdjP headed by important with one dependent). Subjects, complements, modifiers, and determiners are all kinds of dependents.

I will often shorten Dependent to Dep.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Subject
passages.heading: Subject
passages.text:
(a type of Dependent)

- The subject typically functions as the topic of the clause and is syntactically a dependent of the VP in a clause.

- It most often precedes the VP in declarative clauses (e.g., A sunny day [should be savoured]).

- In questions with subject-auxiliary inversion, the subject follows the auxiliary (e.g., Could they be right?), a structure further explored in Section.

- Semantically, the subject often denotes the agent in active sentences, but this is not universally the case, setting the stage for exceptions discussed in Section.

- Agreement with the verb in person and number is a key characteristic of the subject, particularly visible in present tense verbs (e.g., I like it vs She likes it).

- While typically an NP, subjects can also be clauses, VPs, or even preposition phrases (PPs), highlighting the flexibility of this grammatical function.

I will often shorten Subject to Subj.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Determiner
passages.heading: Determiner
passages.text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Determiners are found only in NPs.

- A Head noun will never have more than one determiner
(e.g., *my the class).

- Determiners are usually determinative phrases (DPs) or possessive NPs
(e.g., the, almost all, some, each, my, Brett's).

- A singular countable noun needs a determiner to form an NP
(e.g., I need a/the/each/every pen, but not *I need pen).

- Determiners come before modifiers in the NP
(e.g., the Trudeau government).

I will often shorten Determiner to Det. Determiner and modifier are syntactic functions. The -er tells you it's a function. In contrast, Determinative and adjective are lexical categories.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Modifier
passages.heading: Modifier
passages.text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Most heads allow one or more modifiers.

- Typical modifiers are AdjPs and AdvPs, but almost any phrase can be a modifier.

- Modifiers can often change position
(e.g., he quickly jumped the fence, he jumped the fence quickly).

- Modifiers can often be removed without drastic or unexpected changes of meaning (e.g., a nice, furry dog was sitting there a dog was sitting there).

- Similarly, modifiers can almost always be added or removed without affecting grammaticality.

- In theory, the number of modifiers is unlimited.

- Unfortunately, the boundary between complements and modifiers can be very fuzzy.

I will often shorten Modifier to Mod.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Complement
passages.heading: Complement
passages.text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Complements "complete" a phrase. This meaning is reflected in the spelling complement. (Compliments, in contrast, are nice things you say about somebody.)

- Almost any kind of phrase can be a complement, though AdvP and DP complements are very rare.

- Complements usually follow their head. In contrast, modifiers can often move around (e.g., the modifier quickly can go at the beginning, middle, and end of she changed direction).

- Complements are selected by a head
(e.g., listen to the music not *listen about the music; listen selects a to PP.).

- If a phrase is indifferent about a head, it's not a complement. For example, on Monday can go with almost any verb
(e.g., eat/play/work/think/sit on Monday).

- There are zero, one, two, or sometimes three complements, but never four. (If you think of subjects as a kind of complement, then four is possible.)

- Unfortunately, the boundary between complements and modifiers can be very fuzzy.

I will often shorten Complement to Comp.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object
passages.heading: Object
passages.text:
(a type of Complement)

- Objects are a kind of complement.

- Objects are always dependents in VPs or in PPs
(e.g., grab the bag or in the bag).

- Objects are almost always NPs.

- If there's an action, the object is usually what is acted upon
(e.g., I threw the ball).

- The object of an active sentence is usually the subject of the related passive sentence (e.g., A friend made the cake the cake was made by a friend).

- Verbs like be, become, seem, appear, feel (e.g., I feel good), look (e.g., it looks easy), etc. take complements, but not the special kind called objects.

I will often shorten Object to Obj.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object > Box: Exercises
passages.heading: Exercises
passages.text:
Underline the whole subject in each quotation. If there is more than one subject, underline the first two. Ignore any further subjects.

- Undressing her was an act of recklessness, a kind of vandalism, like releasing a zoo full of animals, or blowing up a dam. xxxxxxxxxxxxxx Chabon

- The war in Zagreb began over a pack of cigarettes.Nović

- Jack put his arm out the window, waving his hat like a visiting dignitary, backed into the street, and floated away, gentling the gleaming dirigible through the shadows of arching elm trees, light dropping on it through their leaves like confetti as it made its ceremonious passage. xxxxxxxx Robinson

- A sudden warm rainstorm washes down in sweet hyphens.xxxxxxxx M. Ledger

- And as the ax bites into the wood, be comforted in the fact that the ache in your heart and the confusion in your soul means that you are still alive, still human, and still open to the beauty of the world, even though you have done nothing to deserve it.Harding

- Within seconds of that thought, the train entered Washington, where she was to come to her end more than sixty-eight years later, a mother to seven living and two dead, a grandmother to twenty-one living and three dead, a great-grandmother to twelve, a great-great grandmother to twins.P. Jones

- We were all a little drunk with spring, like the fat bees reeling from flower to flower, and a strange insurrectionary current ran among us.Wolff

- When he was dry, he believed it was alcohol he needed, but when he had a few drinks in him, he knew it was something else, possibly a woman; and when he had it all - cash, booze, and a wife - he couldn't be distracted from the great emptiness that was always falling through him and never hit the ground.Johnson

- Lizards skit like quick beige sticks.Beard

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object
passages.heading: Object
passages.text:
Answer key

- Undressing her

- The war

- Jack it

- A sudden warm rainstorm

- the ax the ache in your heart and the confusion in your soul

- the train she

- We a strange insurrectionary current

- he he

- Lizards

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases
passages.heading: Adjectives and their phrases
passages.text:
Now that you have a better understanding of categories and verbs, it's time to look at the characteristics of adjectives and adjective phrases.

Before we start, I think it's useful to restate that these characteristics are often related to teaching points. For example, the issue of nouns being countable is actually an issue that causes a great deal of confusion for students. So, with that said,

- Adjectives typically denote properties or characteristics, but not objects or actions.

- Adjectives always head adjective phrases.

- Adjective phrases typically function as modifiers in NPs (a nice day) and complements in VPs (was nice). Sometimes they also function as modifiers in AdjPs, though not in VPs or most other phrase types, and complements in PPs (for free).

- Any phrase that can function as complement to all three of be, and become, and seem is an adjective phrase.

- Any word that can be modified by all three of very, and too, and so is an adjective (or an adverb).

- Many adjectives can take -er and -est endings, but so can a number of commonly used adverbs.

- Many adjectives can be graded with more and less, and most and least, but so can many adverbs.

- Many adjectives can take an -ly ending and become adverbs.

Here are some examples.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Denotation
passages.heading: Denotation
passages.text:
As in Section, the terms used here are just everyday descriptions. They're not technical terms, nor are they meant to be exhaustive.

- evaluative properties: good, great, helpful, interesting

- scalar properties: big, heavy, high, old, thick

- relationship properties: Canadian, different, mutual, ninth, other

- material properties: ceramic, metallic, plastic, wooden, woolen

- state properties: amorphous, aqueous, crystalline, gaseous, viscous

- etc.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Heading adjective phrases
passages.heading: Heading adjective phrases
passages.text:
- happy, very happy, very happy with the outcome

- nice, not nice, no nicer than the other one

- interesting, easily more interesting than mine

- slightly too big

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases as modifiers in noun phrases
passages.heading: Adjective phrases as modifiers in noun phrases
path: Adjective phrases as modifiers in noun phrases
passages.text: - civil rights, some quite good news, national security, the whole thing, recent years, our mental health, her very best friend, a really big deal, a supremely important responsibility

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases as complements to be, become, and seem
passages.heading: Adjective phrases as complements to be, become, and seem
path: Adjective phrases as complements to be, become, and seem
passages.text: - is/became/seems clear, aware, available, pregnant, familiar to me, popular with the ladies, and very interested in the difference

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases with very, too, and so as modifiers
passages.heading: Adjective phrases with very, too, and so as modifiers
path: Adjective phrases with very, too, and so as modifiers
passages.text:
- very/too/so good, important, late, different, difficult, and nice

- (Many adverbs work here too: well, importantly, differently, nicely)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Taking -er and -est suffixes
passages.heading: Taking -er and -est suffixes
passages.text:
- bigger, biggest; greater, greatest; older, oldest; lower, lowest; younger, youngest

- (A number of commonly used adverbs work here too: stay longer, leave earlier, run faster, work harder, jump higher)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Graded with more and less, and most and least
passages.heading: Graded with more and less, and most and least
path: Graded with more and less, and most and least
passages.text:
- more/less/most/least likely, expensive, favourite, important, restrictive, effective, able, possible

- (Again, adverbs like frequently, environmentally, politically, often, etc. work here too)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Take -ly to form adverbs
passages.heading: Take -ly to form adverbs
passages.text: - quick quickly; real really; actual actually; probable probably; final finally; simple simply; exact exactly; certain certainly

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise
passages.heading: Exercise
passages.text:
Apply the properties to identify the adjectives in this excerpt from A very old man with enormous wings by Gabriel García Márquez, translated by Gregory Rabassa. If you're not sure how to do this, have a look at the worked answers that follow.

On the following day, everyone knew that a flesh-and-blood angel was held captive in Pelayo's house. Against the judgment of the wise neighbor woman, for whom angels in those times were the fugitive survivors of a celestial conspiracy, they did not have the heart to club him to death. Pelayo watched over him all afternoon from the kitchen, armed with his bailiff's club, and before going to bed he dragged him out of the mud and locked him up with the hens in the wire chicken coop. In the middle of the night, when the rain stopped, Pelayo and Elisenda were still killing crabs. A short time afterward, the child woke up without a fever and with a desire to eat. Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas. But when they went out into the courtyard with the first light of dawn, they found the whole neighborhood in front of the chicken coop having fun with the angel, without the slightest reverence, tossing him things to eat through the openings in the wire as if he weren't a supernatural creature but a circus animal.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Following
passages.heading: Following
passages.text:
On the following day everyone knew that a flesh-and-blood angel was held captive in Pelayo's house.

- Following is a modifier in the noun phrase the following day, but it is not modifiable by very, so, or too.

- There is no more/most/less/least following.

- We can't say something is the followingest.

- There is no adverb followingly.

- It can't function as the complement of become or seem
(e.g., the day became following).

Therefore, following is probably not an adjective, or at best it is a very bad example of an adjective. Since it ends in -ing, consider whether it might be a verb.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Those
passages.heading: Those
passages.text:
Against the judgement of the wise neighbor woman, for whom angels in those times were the fugitive survivors of a celestial conspiracy, they did not have the heart to club him to death.

- Those comes before the noun times, but it doesn't denote a property. Instead, it has a kind of pointing meaning.

- It is not gradable with -er, more, or less.

- It can't be modified by very.

- It can't function as complement of become or seem.

- There is no word *thosely.

So, those is probably not an adjective either.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Those > Box: Pause and reflect
passages.heading: Pause and reflect
passages.text: Pattern recognition: Notice how we've now seen two words (following and those) that seem like they might be adjectives but fail the diagnostic tests. What does this tell us about the importance of using multiple tests rather than relying on just one criterion?

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Wire
passages.heading: Wire
passages.text:
Pelayo watched over him all afternoon from the kitchen, armed with his bailiff's club, and before going to bed he dragged him out of the mud and locked him up with the hens in the wire chicken coop.

- Wire functions as a modifier in the noun phrase the wire chicken coop, but it denotes a physical object, not a property.

- We can say that seems like wire, but not *that seems wire.

- It has none of the other characteristics of an adjective.

- It can modify a verb (e.g., a wire wrapped guitar string).

Again, wire is probably not an adjective. Given that it denotes a physical object, consider whether it might be a noun.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Magnanimous
passages.heading: Magnanimous
passages.text:
Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas.

- Magnanimous doesn't modify a noun, but it functions as a complement in a VP headed by felt.

- It denotes a characteristic of the people (they).

- There isn't a word *magnanimouser, but we do have more or less magnanimous.

- It can be modified by very.

- There is an adverb magnanimously.

- There's nothing wrong with it seems magnanimous.

So, magnanimous seems to be an adjective.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Fresh
passages.heading: Fresh
passages.text:
Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas.

- Fresh is a modifier in the noun phrase fresh water.

- It denotes a scalar property.

- It can be graded (e.g., fresher, freshest, least fresh).

- Something can be very fresh.

- There is an adverb freshly.

- Something can seem fresh. And while it's kind of unusual for something to become fresh, just because things tend to go from fresh to stale and not the other way around, there's no grammatical problem with become fresh.

Clearly, fresh is an adjective.

Now, have another look at the passage with these examples in mind.

Summary and review
Fill in the gaps with noun(s), verb(s), or adjective(s).

a

zzzA word that denotes a person, place, or thing is a/an [gap].
[gap] It can typically follow the or a. A word that is countable is a/an [gap].
Almost every [gap] can follow the infinitive marker to.
A noun phrase is typically headed by a/an [gap].
An adjective phrase is always headed by a/an [gap].
[gap] phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object. Any phrase that can function as a complement to be, become, and seem is
a/an [gap] phrase. Almost every [gap] has an -ing form.
[gap] commonly denote actions and states, and they do not denote physical objects.

zzzMany [gap] can take -er and -est endings, but so can many adverbs.
[gap] typically denote properties or characteristics, but not objects or actions.
[gap] phrases can typically be possessive. Typically,
[gap] can be modified by adjective phrases. Many [gap] can take an -ly ending and become adverbs. Almost every [gap] has a third-person singular -s form. A/an [gap] is often in an agreement relationship with a phrase functioning as its subject. A/an [gap] can't be modified by an adjective phrase. The head of a clause is typically a/an [gap] phrase.
[gap] can denote almost anything from objects to actions to characteristics.

zzzAny word that can be modified by very, too, and so is a/an [gap] (or an adverb).
[gap] can't typically be modified by adverb phrases. A word that has a past-tense form is a/an [gap]. Many [gap] can be gradable with less and least, and so can many adverbs.
[gap] phrases modify nouns and [gap], but not verbs. Almost any word that is plural is a/an [gap].
A [gap] phrase must be headed by a verb.

a

zzzKeep working on recalling the properties of nouns and verbs, and add adjectives to your practice.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Pronouns
passages.heading: Pronouns
passages.text:
- Did you ever notice that we sometimes say things like, look at the baby. It's so cute. But we'd never say, look at her boyfriend. *It's so cute.

- Did you ever notice that plural they is used to refer to anything from teachers to trees but singular they is only for persons? If you doubt me, give it a try.

- Did you ever notice that in some uses, whose is used to refer to anything from children to chambers (e.g., The chasm ran through a chamber whose ceiling was lost in the darkness above), but who is only for persons?

- Did you ever notice that in some cases which can't be used for persons (e.g., *that's the guy which sold me the car)?

- Of course, everyone's aware of the masculine/feminine gender pronouns, but did you ever think about personal/non-personal gender pronouns?

Pronouns are a special kind of noun. They typically refer to another noun phrase that has come before or that comes later (e.g., in Xi saw the egg fall but caught it before it smashed, the pronoun it refers to the egg). There are only a small number of pronouns. The main ones are set out in Table.

The main pronouns in Modern English

A dummy pronoun is one which does not refer, as in There are two dummy pronouns or It's windy today. There is no semantic reason for there and it to appear in those sentences. Their role is purely grammatical, because English demands a subject in this context.

For discussion of interrogative and relative pronouns, see Sections and.

When it comes to the possessive pronouns, gender agreement can be an issue. In some languages, they agree in gender with the head noun, while in English, his, hers, and its agree with the possessor. So, for the grandmother of a man, English would use the masculine his because he is a man, while French would use the feminine sa because the grandmother is a woman.

[]It's his grandmother. [masculine] C'est sa grand-mère.[feminine]

[]It's his grandfather...[masculine] C'est son grand-père.[masculine]

[]It's her grandmother...[feminine] C'est sa grand-mère.[feminine]

[]It's her grandfather.....[feminine] C'est son grand-père.[masculine]

This can be a challenging concept to adjust to if the language you grew up speaking (L1) makes the opposite choice. Even for speakers whose L1 doesn't make any such grammatical distinction, it may be worth pointing out how English gender agreement works in such cases.

One interesting property of pronouns is that only a pronoun (but not all pronouns) can appear in a tag question, such as that's very interesting, isn't it? or there's more, isn't there?

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases
passages.heading: Determinatives and determinative phrases
passages.text:
There is a good deal of confusion about the terms determiner and determinative. The former is a function and the latter a category. Unfortunately, some books use them the other way around. (Those using them flipped around include A comprehensive grammar of English, English and Simple English Wiktionary, and Wikipedia.) I will use determiner as a function (like modifier) and determinative as a category (like adjective). In general, a reliable dictionary for checking whether a word is a determinative is the Simple English Wiktionary, (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Category:Determiners) although, confusingly, it calls them "determiners". It will help if you can keep the following terminological reminder in mind.

- Determiner and modifier are syntactic functions. The -er tells you it's a function.

- Determinative and adjective are lexical categories.

For the most part, determinative phrases (DPs) (The term DP here refers simply to phrases headed by determinatives (e.g., some, many more, less than five). This differs from how some linguists use "DP" to refer to what we call noun phrases.) tend to be single-word phrases. Occasionally, though, a DP includes a modifier. In the following examples, the modifiers are italicized.

- more people, many days, these sandwiches, every time, least two times

The function that DPs typically perform is that of determiner. Only NPs have determiners, and a head noun may only have one determiner (the ball) or none (balls), never two or more (*the my ball). A singular countable noun typically requires a determiner to be a full NP, and without one, it is usually ungrammatical (e.g., *she passed me ball).

Determinatives also commonly function as modifiers as in I feel better, enough, good, etc.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases > Determinatives vs. adjectives
passages.heading: Determinatives vs. adjectives
passages.text:
In traditional English grammar, there is no concept of determiner or determinative. There was a special category for the and a called articles, but all the other determinatives were just called adjectives when they preceded a noun. For instance, the word some in some people is categorized as an adjective in the Merriam-Webster dictionary, even though it has none of the other characteristics of adjectives. There are two reasonably good ways to distinguish whether a word is a determinative or an adjective.

- Most adjectives are happy to follow the, but it's quite rare for determinatives to do so.

- Most determinatives can function in the frame of the Noun (e.g., some of the people, all of the time, each of the tests, many of the changes), but adjectives never do.

And, of course, you can simply consult the list of determinatives in the Simple English Wiktionary (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Category:Determiners) or the appendix because there aren't very many. A sample is presented in Table.

Table: A sample of determinatives in Modern English

Only when used without a head noun (e.g., What person is this? but not What is this? for a person.) The demonstratives are not always impersonal (e.g., This is my friend. or Those who try win.)

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases > Determinatives vs. pronouns
passages.heading: Determinatives vs. pronouns
passages.text: Like pronouns, determinatives can often refer to NPs that appear elsewhere in the discourse (e.g., I bought a bag of chocolate chip cookies, and I ate some on the way home). For this reason, they are often categorized as pronouns. For example, this use of the word some is categorized as a pronoun in the Merriam-Webster dictionary, even though it has none of the other characteristics of pronouns. For one thing, determinatives don't have the various forms that most pronouns have and, like adjectives, pronouns can't appear in the frame of the Noun, shown above (e.g., *she of the students or *us of the teachers).

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Definite vs. indefinite
passages.heading: Definite vs. indefinite
passages.text:
A given NP is either definite or indefinite. In English, this is usually marked by the choice of determiner (the function). Certain determinatives, such as the and this along with possessive (genitive) NPs, such as my and Brett's, mark the NP as definite. Other determinatives such as a, any, and some mark the NP as indefinite, and most NPs without a determiner are also indefinite. The main exceptions here are proper names and most pronouns, which are definite.

But there's a subtlety here. Determiner choice is a grammatical fact about the NP. Whether the speaker expects the listener to identify the referent is a semantic one. The two usually align, but not always: generic the (the lion is a noble beast) is grammatically definite but doesn't pick out a specific lion, while specific a (a certain student kept disrupting class) is grammatically indefinite but does.

Definiteness is one of those concepts that seems obvious at first blush but which, on second thought, turns out to be less clear. A useful if somewhat simplified way to think about it is found in CGEL, which says definiteness can be understood as pre-empting a question with which?

Compare, for example:

-

- Where did you park the car?

- The father of one of my students rang me up last night.

- The first person to run the mile in under four minutes was Roger Bannister.

Example (i) illustrates the frequent case where the addressee can be assumed to be familiar with the referent of the definite NP: you have been driving the car and presumably know a good deal more about it than that it is a car -what colour and make of car it is, and so on. You thus don't need to ask Which car?: you know which one I'm referring to.
(Huddleston et al., 2002)

(If you did respond to my utterance of [2i] by asking Which one?, it would indicate that there had been a breakdown in communication, that I had been mistaken in assuming that you'd be able to identify which car I was referring to.)

Definiteness can be a trouble point for learners of English because definiteness in English is always marked (by a definite determiner or by virtue of being a pronoun or a proper name), but only optionally marked in many languages. Determiner choice is also only partially conditioned by definiteness. Countability, quantity, number, proximity, and possession all factor in.

Figure: The determinatives and their semantics

From "The love of a good woman" by Alice Munro

Each NP is bracketed (there are some smaller NPs inside these, but we'll ignore them for now). If there is a phrase that functions as Det in an NP, underline it.

a

[These women] aren't so much older than [Kath] and [Sonje]. But [they]'ve reached [a stage in [life] that [Kath] and [Sonje] dread]. [They] turn [the whole beach] into [a platform]. [Their burdens], [their strung-out progeny and maternal poundage], [their authority], can annihilate [the bright water], [the perfect small cove with [the red-limbed arbutus trees]], [the cedars growing crookedly out of [the high rocks]]. [Kath] feels [their threat] particularly, since [she]'s [a mother] now [herself]. When [she] nurses [her baby] [she] often reads [a book], sometimes smokes [a cigarette], so as not to sink into [a sludge of [animal function]]. And [she]'s nursing so that [she] can shrink [her uterus] and flatten [her stomach], not just provide [the baby] - [Noelle] - with [precious maternal antibodies].

xxx[Kath] and [Sonje] have [their own Thermos of [coffee]] and [their extra towels, rigged up as [a shelter] for [Noelle]]. [They] have [their cigarettes] and [their books]. [Sonje] has [a book by [Howard Fast]]. [Her husband] has told [her] that, if [she] has to read [fiction], [she] should read [Fast]. [Kath] is reading [the short stories of [Katherine Mansfield]] and [the short stories of [D. H. Lawrence]]. [Sonje] has got into [the habit of putting down [[her] own book] and picking up [whichever book of [Kath's] that [Kath] is not reading at [the moment]]]. [She] limits [herself] to [one story] and then goes back to [Howard Fast].

When one label names two things

In Chapter 1, you saw that furniture refers to individual items but is grammatically non-count. There's a syntactic property called countability - takes numerals, pluralizes, requires a determiner with singulars - and a semantic property also called countability, whether the referent consists of distinct individuals. They usually agree; when they don't, confusion follows.

This pattern (one label, two overlapping categories) recurs throughout linguistics. We've just seen it with definiteness: syntactic marking by determiners and semantic identifiability usually align but can diverge. We'll meet it again with tense and time in Chapter 5, where a past-tense form can carry present meaning (I wish I knew). It shows up with aspect, gender, and more. The large overlap makes the two look like one; the mismatches then look like exceptions.

When a reference book's rule seems to have too many exceptions, check whether it's conflating a syntactic property with a semantic one. The exceptions are usually just cases where the two don't align. Seeing this is one of the most practical things analytical training gives you: you stop blaming the language for being irregular and start noticing that the description was muddled.

tableSyntax-semantics mismatches

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Noun phrases as determiners
passages.heading: Noun phrases as determiners
passages.text:
If you look back at the Munro passage, you'll notice that most of the determiners you italicized aren't determinatives. They're possessive NPs: their, her, his, Kath's. This is the other major category that fills the determiner slot.

The dependent genitive pronouns in Table - my, your, his, her, its, our, their - are all NPs. They're nouns (specifically pronouns), and they function as determiners, just as the and some do. The difference is purely categorical: the is a determinative; my is a pronoun.

Longer possessive NPs work the same way. In my mother's sister, the NP my mother's functions as a determiner in the larger NP. And my mother's has its own internal structure: the pronoun my functions as a determiner inside it. So we have an NP nested inside an NP, each with its own determiner:

- mother'ssister - my is Det in my mother's; my mother's is Det in my mother's sister

- dog'sowner - the is Det in the dog's; the dog's is Det in the dog's owner

- morning'sheadlines - this is Det in this morning's; this morning's is Det in this morning's headlines

This nesting can go further. In my uncle's dog's vet, the possessive NP my uncle's is a determiner in the NP my uncle's dog's, which is itself a determiner in my uncle's dog's vet. In principle, there's no limit, though in practice we rarely go beyond two levels. Tree diagrams for these structures appear in Figure.

Notice how this connects to the many-to-many theme from the start of the chapter. The determiner function can be filled by a DP (the, some, every) or by a possessive NP (my, Brett's, the dog's). Two different categories, one function.

passages.anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Summary
passages.heading: Summary
passages.text: Most of this analytical detail stays behind the scenes in a language classroom. But it informs every professional judgment you make: whether a textbook's explanation will cause confusion down the road, what examples you choose, and what you say when a student asks you what an adjective is. You don't need to cover all the exceptions and edge cases from the beginning, but you shouldn't be setting traps for yourself to fall into later.
